skill.name: extract-from-pdfs
skill.description: Extract structured datasets from scientific PDF collections for systematic reviews, meta-analyses, and research databases.
skill.summary: Extract structured research data from PDFs, validate results, and export analysis-ready datasets.
skill.author: brunoasm

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer found many patterns, but most high weak-cryptography, Ruby backtick, and sensitive-file findings are false positives from Markdown, schema text, or normal export code. Medium risk remains because the skill intentionally reads local PDFs, writes datasets, uses API credentials, sends research content to model and validation services, and documents an optional pipe-to-shell installer.

medium_findings.title: Remote Installer Piped To Shell
medium_findings.description: The setup guide recommends running a downloaded Ollama installer directly through sh. This is a supply-chain risk if the remote script or transport is compromised, although it is documentation for an optional local model backend and not executed by the skill automatically.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell pattern is explicitly present in the setup guide. Confidence is high for the risky pattern, but lower for exploitability because it requires a user to run an optional install command.

medium_findings.title: Scientific Data Sent To Model And Validation APIs
medium_findings.description: The skill sends titles, abstracts, and PDF-derived content to Anthropic or local Ollama endpoints, and can query GBIF, WFO, GeoNames, Nominatim, PubChem, and NCBI. This is core functionality, not covert exfiltration, but users must understand that research content and extracted fields may leave the local machine when API backends are enabled.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code contains direct model API calls and direct requests.get calls to named external services. The surrounding workflow documentation confirms these calls are intended validation and extraction behavior.

medium_findings.title: Broad Local File Reads And Writes For Research Artifacts
medium_findings.description: The pipeline reads user-provided metadata, schemas, annotations, and PDFs, copies PDFs when requested, and writes JSON, CSV, Excel, SQLite, Python, R, SQL, and report outputs. These operations match the stated workflow but can overwrite user-selected output paths and process sensitive PDFs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are visible in the scripts and are required for the pipeline. The risk is operational rather than malicious because paths are provided through CLI arguments and no hidden locations are targeted by code.

low_findings.title: Environment Variables Used For API Credentials
low_findings.description: The scripts read ANTHROPIC_API_KEY and GEONAMES_USERNAME from environment variables. This is a standard credential handling pattern, and no evidence shows these values are logged or sent to unrelated endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment-variable reads are explicit and limited to expected API configuration. I found no evidence of credential printing or unrelated network transmission.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static scanner flagged many schema, README, and script lines as weak cryptography, but reviewed examples show ordinary words such as method, model, schema fields, and scientific extraction instructions. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or other weak cryptographic algorithms being used for security decisions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review of representative flagged areas showed schema and prompt terminology, not cryptographic code. Confidence is high for dismissal, though I did not manually inspect every duplicate static location.

low_findings.title: Ruby Backtick Findings Are Markdown Code Fences
low_findings.description: The static scanner reported Ruby shell backtick execution across Markdown files. Reviewed instances are Markdown fenced code blocks and inline command examples, not Ruby source execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation fences and command snippets. They are not inside Ruby files and are not executed by the skill itself.

low_findings.title: Sensitive File Pattern Findings Are Mostly Benign Context Matches
low_findings.description: Static findings for Windows SAM databases, certificate files, and SQLite files are context matches on words such as sample, key, batch identifiers, and documented SQLite export. No evidence found that the skill reads system credential stores or private key files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Representative flagged lines show research criteria, batch IDs, and normal SQLite output creation. The conclusion is limited to reviewed evidence and does not claim every static match was individually inspected.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Installer Piped To Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: The setup guide recommends running a downloaded Ollama installer directly through sh. This is a supply-chain risk if the remote script or transport is compromised, although it is documentation for an optional local model backend and not executed by the skill automatically.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell pattern is explicitly present in the setup guide. Confidence is high for the risky pattern, but lower for exploitability because it requires a user to run an optional install command.

content.user_title: Extract Structured Data From Scientific PDFs
content.value_statement: Research teams often need consistent datasets from many scientific PDFs. This skill guides extraction, validation, and export into analysis-ready files.
actual_capabilities: Organizes paper metadata from BibTeX, RIS, directories, or DOI lists.
Filters abstracts with Claude models or a local Ollama backend.
Extracts structured data from PDFs using configurable JSON schemas.
Repairs and validates extracted JSON against a schema.
Enriches extracted fields with scientific databases such as GBIF, GeoNames, PubChem, and NCBI.
Exports cleaned data to JSON, CSV, Excel, SQLite, Python, or R formats.
limitations: Requires user-provided schemas and domain criteria for accurate extraction.
Cloud model backends may send PDFs, abstracts, and extracted data to external APIs.
Validation API coverage is strongest for supported taxonomy, geography, chemistry, and gene use cases.
Quality metrics require manually annotated ground truth examples.

use_cases.title: Build A Systematic Review Dataset
use_cases.description: Convert a library of research PDFs into structured records for screening, extraction, and meta-analysis.

use_cases.title: Create A Domain Research Database
use_cases.description: Extract repeated observations, measurements, or study attributes into a reusable database.

use_cases.title: Validate Extraction Quality
use_cases.description: Sample papers, add ground truth annotations, and calculate precision, recall, and F1 metrics.

prompt_templates.title: Start A Small Extraction
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a small PDF collection.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me extract structured data from 10 scientific PDFs. Ask me for the research goal, PDF organization, and fields to extract.

prompt_templates.title: Design An Extraction Schema
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a schema before running the extraction scripts.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a domain-specific extraction schema for my systematic review. Include objective, instructions, output fields, and validation notes.

prompt_templates.title: Run The Full Pipeline
prompt_templates.scenario: Execute metadata organization, filtering, extraction, repair, validation, and export.
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through the complete PDF extraction pipeline using my metadata file, schema, and preferred export format.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Extraction Quality
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow for measuring accuracy against manual annotations.
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare a validation set, define annotation guidance, and calculate precision, recall, and F1 for each extracted field.

output_examples.input: A folder of ecology PDFs and a schema for plant visitor observations.
output_examples.output: A cleaned dataset with paper identifiers, plant species, locations, observation methods, visitor lists, validation status, and export files for analysis.

output_examples.input: A systematic review library with abstracts and full-text PDFs.
output: A filtered paper list with inclusion reasoning.
A structured extraction file for relevant papers.
A validation report showing field-level precision and recall.

output_examples.input: Extracted records that need taxonomy and geography checks.
output_examples.output: An enriched dataset with standardized names, matched external identifiers, coordinates when available, and notes for unmatched values.

best_practices: Start with two or three representative PDFs before processing the full collection.
Use a precise schema with required fields, examples, and rules for missing values.
Run validation on a manually annotated sample before relying on final metrics.
anti_patterns: Do not send confidential PDFs to cloud APIs without approval from the data owner.
Do not use generic extraction prompts when the review has strict inclusion criteria.
Do not publish extracted datasets without reviewing validation errors and sampled source evidence.

faq.question: What types of PDFs does this skill target?
faq.answer: It targets scientific papers that contain structured observations, measurements, outcomes, or study metadata.

faq.question: Can it work without cloud API calls?
faq.answer: Abstract filtering can use local Ollama. PDF extraction scripts are designed around Claude API access.

faq.question: Does it include a ready-made schema?
faq.answer: It includes templates and an ecology example. Users should customize fields for each research domain.

faq.question: Can it validate extracted data?
faq.answer: Yes. It can call supported scientific databases and calculate metrics against manual ground truth annotations.

faq.question: Which export formats are supported?
faq.answer: The scripts support JSON, CSV, Excel, SQLite, Python pandas files, and R data files.

faq.question: What should users review before running it?
faq.answer: Users should review API usage, data privacy, output paths, schema instructions, and validation requirements.
